title: Platinum Artifact Collection And Analysis Dashboard
strapline: A dashboard-first intake and decision cockpit for current games and future additions: gather source artifacts, analyze them, turn them into a Platinum-focused instantiation plan, and keep conformance work grounded in durable evidence.
currentGoal: Make artifact collection and analysis the front door for every game on Platinum, with Aurora and Galaxy Guardians visible today and Windigo Invaders entering through a measured, source-first intake path.
dashboardRead: This page should be the primary place where new game materials are added, assessed, and turned into explicit build decisions. It should stay compact at the top level, let us click into detail by game, and keep showing what source families are still missing before design outruns evidence.

phaseDefinitions.label: Artifact Collection
phaseDefinitions.purpose: Accession real source materials first: gameplay video, manuals, score tables, stills, cabinet art, and written reference pages.

phaseDefinitions.label: Analysis
phaseDefinitions.purpose: Turn sources into reviewable windows and evidence: contact sheets, waveforms, semantic notes, timing slices, and confidence gaps.

phaseDefinitions.label: Platinum Instantiation Plan
phaseDefinitions.purpose: Convert evidence into an explicit game plan that separates Platinum-owned host work from game-owned rules, timing, audio, and identity.

phaseDefinitions.label: Implementation
phaseDefinitions.purpose: Build only the smallest evidence-backed slice needed to prove the game on Platinum.

phaseDefinitions.label: Conformance
phaseDefinitions.purpose: Measure the game against its source lineage with runtime artifacts, personas, and visible review surfaces.

phaseDefinitions.label: Release Review
phaseDefinitions.purpose: Decide whether the game is ready for public claims, beta review, or continued private iteration.

artifactFamilies.label: Gameplay video
artifactFamilies.detail: Longplay, attract, operator, and targeted scenario windows with clear provenance and usable audio.
artifactFamilies.why: This is the main source for enemy movement, player pacing, score behavior, wave progression, and audio-event timing.
examples: One full or near-full longplay with original audio
One attract-mode or title/start sequence clip
One focused clip showing a single stage or scenario clearly
artifactFamilies.minimum: Enough to see the first 2-3 minutes clearly, hear real game audio, and study at least one representative stress window.

artifactFamilies.label: Manuals and rule documents
artifactFamilies.detail: Operator sheets, score tables, cabinet docs, and written descriptions of mechanics and progression.
artifactFamilies.why: These stop us from guessing scoring, bonus rules, player counts, lives, progression, and cabinet-facing messaging.
examples: Written gameplay overview from a reliable arcade reference
artifactFamilies.minimum: At least one trustworthy source for score values and one source for rules or operator-facing setup.

artifactFamilies.label: Still-image references
artifactFamilies.detail: Cabinet, bezel, title, score-table, sprite-family, cannon/ship, bunker, and HUD references.
artifactFamilies.why: Still images let us study visual identity without motion blur and are usually the fastest way to verify UI, sprite families, colors, and cabinet framing.
artifactFamilies.minimum: At least one clear title/HUD image plus one image set covering the main player and enemy families.

artifactFamilies.label: Audio references
artifactFamilies.detail: Isolated or cleaner windows for cue identity, tempo, density, and game-event timing.
artifactFamilies.why: Gameplay audio quality depends on hearing what happens between events, not just proving that a cue exists.
examples: Pressure or march loop windows with minimal commentary
artifactFamilies.minimum: At least one usable start/end phrase and one representative live-play scene with real pressure and fire density.

artifactFamilies.label: Semantic timing windows
artifactFamilies.detail: Event-labeled clips for start, fire, hit, descent, failure, transition, and results moments.
artifactFamilies.why: These become the contract slices we tune against when timing, pacing, or transitions drift.
examples: First enemy descent or pressure moment
artifactFamilies.minimum: At least 4-6 labeled clips covering start, pressure, hit, loss, and end-state behavior.

artifactFamilies.label: Runtime correspondence captures
artifactFamilies.detail: Persona-driven or manual recordings from Platinum that can be reviewed against the reference set.
artifactFamilies.why: Without Platinum-side comparison captures, we can’t tell whether the implementation is getting closer or merely changing.
artifactFamilies.minimum: At least one manual and one harness-driven capture once runtime work begins.

minimumIntakeChecklist.title: One authoritative longplay video
minimumIntakeChecklist.why: Needed to anchor pacing, wave structure, scoring rhythm, and live soundscape.
minimumIntakeChecklist.goodEnough: A preserved source package with checksum, manifest, contact sheet, and waveform.

minimumIntakeChecklist.title: One rules or operator source
minimumIntakeChecklist.why: Needed to keep lives, scoring, player count, and progression from becoming guesswork.
minimumIntakeChecklist.goodEnough: A manual page, operator sheet, or high-confidence rules page saved into the repo.

minimumIntakeChecklist.title: One score-table source
minimumIntakeChecklist.why: Needed to define exact hit values, bonus structure, and any special scoring behavior.
minimumIntakeChecklist.goodEnough: A still or document source with readable values and enough provenance to trust it.

minimumIntakeChecklist.title: One visual identity set
minimumIntakeChecklist.why: Needed to establish title, HUD, sprite families, player ship/cannon, and cabinet-facing presentation.
minimumIntakeChecklist.goodEnough: A small bundle of readable stills covering title, HUD, main enemies, and player object.

minimumIntakeChecklist.title: One event-labeled timing bundle
minimumIntakeChecklist.why: Needed so the first runtime slice can target real start, pressure, hit, loss, and end-state windows.
minimumIntakeChecklist.goodEnough: A short set of named clips or notes pointing to exact timestamps in preserved video.

globalDocs.label: Classic Arcade Ingestion Framework
globalDocs.detail: Canonical source-first intake process for future games.

globalDocs.label: Game Conformance Catalog
globalDocs.detail: Cross-game maintained index for enemies, audio, stages, and personas.

globalDocs.label: Reference Media Inventory
globalDocs.detail: Shared media inventory and source-of-truth reference ledger.

globalDocs.label: Platinum Audio Conformance Framework
globalDocs.detail: Generic audio intake, scene review, and live-mix framework for any future game.

globalDocs.label: Motion Grammar Vocabulary
globalDocs.detail: Shared movement vocabulary for source ingestion, routeability checks, and candidate promotion across games.

globalHunts: Find manual and score-table sources early so scoring, bonus, and staging do not remain implied.
Prefer at least one longplay plus one cleaner short-window video per game line.
Promote audio windows that match real gameplay scenes, not only isolated cue existence.
Keep every game's plan explicit about what Platinum owns versus what the game owns.
If a missing artifact family repeatedly blocks conformance, add it to the standing intake requirement set.

platformExtensions.title: Generic preserved-source package contract
platformExtensions.detail: Every new game should enter through the same manifest, checksum, preview-artifact, and README pattern used here for Windigo Invaders.

platformExtensions.title: Generic audio scene and live-mix review
platformExtensions.detail: The new audio scene/live-mix analyzers should remain game-configured so a third game can use them without bespoke platform rewrites.

platformExtensions.title: Persona capture and review rail
platformExtensions.detail: Future games should inherit the same persona-driven capture, review, and replay discipline once their rules are mature enough.

platformExtensions.title: Shared motion grammar and routeability gates
platformExtensions.detail: Movement candidates should be described with common primitives, then promoted only when visual fit, safety, shot windows, and player routeability all hold together.

games.title: Aurora Galactica
games.themeRead: The most mature current game. Intake and conformance should stay visible because future games should inherit the process, not just the runtime.

phaseStatus.summary: Reference and extracted media families are established.
Challenge, sprite, motion, and artifact scoring are already active.
Measured quality gaps are explicit and documented.
Still improving challenge-stage and motion fidelity.
Public family live, `/dev` remains the review lane.

headlineScores.label: Challenge-stage conformance
headlineScores.detail: Still the clearest weak user-facing area.

headlineScores.label: Human-perfect routeability guard
headlineScores.detail: Challenge candidates now fail promotion if they reduce strong-player scoring windows.

games.decisionRead: Aurora should continue using this dashboard as the visible intake and quality cockpit, but the other machine can keep driving the deeper challenge-stage work while this repo retains the authoritative status surface. For Aurora, the highest-value new artifacts are mostly derived windows cut from sources we already have, not necessarily brand-new raw videos.

planTracks.title: Keep Aurora as the process template
planTracks.goal: Future games should inherit Aurora's measurement discipline without inheriting Aurora-specific truth.
deliverables: Keep hosted docs aligned with current conformance artifacts
Preserve audio and motion harnesses as generic platform contracts where possible
Block visually tempting challenge candidates that reduce human-perfect routeability

nextArtifacts.title: Extract one complete labeled subclip for each challenge-stage family under active tuning
nextArtifacts.why: We already have videos of every Aurora challenging stage. The gap is not basic coverage. The gap is that many comparisons still depend on long source videos instead of short, named subclips that isolate the exact set-piece being tuned.
find: For each tuned challenge family, cut one 8-20 second subclip that starts before the first enemy becomes visible and ends after the result hold settles
Include the full open, the full traversal pattern, and the full result banner/score hold in a single named clip
Name each clip by challenge family and visible pattern type, not only by stage number
nextArtifacts.goodEnough: At least one preserved extracted clip for each challenge family currently being tuned, with explicit start/end timestamps and a note explaining what visible set-piece it is meant to prove.

nextArtifacts.title: Promote frame-accurate motion windows for flap cadence and challenge-stage novelty
nextArtifacts.why: Aurora still needs better motion truth for bee/butterfly feel and for why one challenge pattern reads as a distinct spectacle instead of a generic enemy sweep.
find: Extract 2-6 second windows where a single bee or butterfly family is readable for at least 20 consecutive frames
Extract 4-10 second challenge windows where the novelty move is visually obvious, such as a fan, ladder, sweep, or cross-over pattern
Prefer windows with stable framing, minimal overlay obstruction, and no scene cut in the middle of the motion
nextArtifacts.goodEnough: A small reusable set of motion windows where frame-to-frame flap timing, spacing, and direction changes can be reviewed without scrubbing through full longplay footage.

nextArtifacts.title: Promote boss-hit and explosion lifecycle still bundles
nextArtifacts.why: Impact and explosion feedback remains the weakest broad artifact row.
find: One labeled frame sequence for a boss taking its first damaging hit
One labeled frame sequence for a full boss-destruction event
One labeled frame sequence for a normal enemy explosion from onset through decay
If possible, include stills that show exactly when score text does and does not appear during those events
nextArtifacts.goodEnough: At least one readable frame bundle for first-hit boss damage, one for full boss destruction, and one for a normal enemy explosion, each ordered so the onset, peak, and decay are obvious.

previews.label: Rejected challenge candidate before/after
previews.detail: Stage 3 candidate review sheet for a target-control candidate blocked by human-perfect routeability regression.

games.title: Galaxy Guardians
games.lineage: Galaxian-style second-game ingestion proof on Platinum
games.themeRead: The current visible second-game proof. It is strong enough to deserve deeper artifact discipline, but still weak enough that the dashboard should keep missing evidence and risk areas visible.

phaseStatus.summary: Multiple Galaxian-family sources and extracted windows already exist.
Audio, movement, long-surface, and visual identity artifacts are now substantial.
Top-ten and audio sprint plans exist, but deeper play quality still needs work.
Playable preview on Platinum with game-owned identity.
Reference and playtest reads are honest and still below public-release confidence.
Useful as a review artifact, not yet a mature standalone public promise.

headlineScores.label: Public readiness
headlineScores.detail: Still too low for a strong public promise.

headlineScores.label: Movement routeability
headlineScores.detail: New Guardians routeability review; weakest route is stage-five stress.

headlineScores.label: Motion grammar candidates
headlineScores.detail: Shared-grammar movement candidate queue; top item is stage-five routeability relief.

headlineScores.label: Audio reference comparison
headlineScores.detail: Good coverage, still weak direct acoustic proxy fit.

headlineScores.label: Audio live mix
headlineScores.detail: Live mix is now strong; source-faithful match is the remaining gap.

games.decisionRead: Guardians should use this dashboard as the main cross-artifact intake and planning cockpit while deeper runtime quality work continues. The current best return remains midrun fairness plus serious audio conformance work, backed by the new generic audio framework. For Guardians, the highest-value new artifacts are a mix of cleaner source-side audio scenes, deeper-play runtime correspondence captures, and a few tightly defined motion and score-policy windows.

planTracks.title: Audio authenticity sprint
planTracks.goal: Make gameplay sound more source-faithful, not just technically busy.
deliverables: Scene review and live-mix acceptance gates
Shot, dive, hit, and loss family rebuilds against reference windows

planTracks.title: Deeper-play fairness
planTracks.goal: Improve stage-five-and-beyond survivability without restoring fake-safe lanes.
deliverables: Routeability deltas for movement candidates before runtime promotion
Shared motion-grammar candidate rows before runtime tuning

nextArtifacts.title: Promote source-side audio scene windows with direct cabinet audio and named gameplay context
nextArtifacts.why: Guardians now has decent cue coverage, but still needs more source-faithful acoustic match. `Cleaner` here should mean better acoustic usefulness, not just a nicer-looking video.
find: 6-15 second source windows where cabinet audio is dominant and spoken commentary is absent or quiet enough not to mask cue timing
Separate named windows for start, player shot, enemy shot, hit, dive warning, player loss, and game over
At least one 15-30 second live-play scene where the recurring pressure bed is audible between shots and hits
nextArtifacts.goodEnough: One promoted audio-scene window per major cue family plus one promoted live-play pressure scene, each with timestamps and a note explaining what cue family or mix property it is meant to anchor.

nextArtifacts.title: Add comparable stage-five-and-beyond runtime correspondence captures
nextArtifacts.why: The weakest remaining gameplay gap is deeper-play survivability and fairness.
find: At least two persona-driven captures that both reach stage 5 or later under the same general review conditions
At least one manual review capture where lower-field crowding, escape space, and recovery after a near miss are visible
Each capture should preserve score, stage reached, survival time, and whether the player died to crowding, shot density, or a readable mistake
nextArtifacts.goodEnough: Two or more directly comparable stage-5+ captures that let us say whether a fairness change reduced cheap-feeling failure or only changed difficulty in the abstract.

nextArtifacts.title: Extract direct pulse and flap motion windows from source footage
nextArtifacts.why: Pulse and flap authority are still felt quality issues.
find: 2-5 second formation-rest windows where aliens are mostly stationary horizontally so pulse amplitude is easy to read
2-5 second motion windows where one alien family remains readable for at least 15-20 consecutive frames
Prefer windows without score overlays, explosions, or overlapping dives masking the target body shapes
nextArtifacts.goodEnough: A small reusable set of short clips where pulse rhythm and flap cadence can be reviewed frame-by-frame without guessing which alien body state belongs to which frame.

nextArtifacts.title: Promote labeled score-popup policy windows
nextArtifacts.why: Score-pop behavior should be driven by visible source cases, not inferred.
find: Short clips where a special-value hit clearly triggers visible score text
Short clips where a normal hit clearly does not trigger visible score text
If possible, one labeled case for each distinct hit-value family we plan to model
nextArtifacts.goodEnough: Enough labeled yes-score-pop and no-score-pop cases that we can write a source-backed rule for when score text should appear after a hit.

previews.label: Stage-five routeability before/after
previews.detail: Analysis-only Guardians candidate chart: baseline routeability 4.5/10, best candidate 5.8/10.

games.title: Windigo Invaders
games.lineage: Space Invaders-derived future Platinum game, themed eventually around the Windigo legend
games.themeRead: This should enter through evidence first. The goal is not to rush a derivative shell, but to build the strongest artifact package and Platinum instantiation plan before design decisions harden.

phaseStatus.summary: Gameplay plus first rules/service source packages are accessioned and preserved.
First contact sheet, waveform, and written rules grounding are now in repo.
The initial Platinum-focused instantiation plan now has written score/rules anchors.
Do not build runtime slices before the initial evidence package is strong enough.
Scorers should come after the first explicit plan and runtime slice.
No public claim yet; this is still in intake.

headlineScores.label: Current phase
headlineScores.detail: The game is intentionally still pre-runtime.

headlineScores.label: Seed source count
headlineScores.detail: Gameplay plus rules/service preserved-source packages are now in-repo.

headlineScores.label: First-pass previews
headlineScores.detail: Contact sheet plus audio waveform generated.

games.decisionRead: The next best spend is on artifact breadth, not code. We should use this dashboard to decide what missing source families matter most before we commit to game-specific runtime assumptions.

planTracks.title: Build the initial Platinum instantiation plan
planTracks.goal: Separate what Platinum will host from what Windigo Invaders must own as game truth.
deliverables: Stage, bunker, cannon, and invader-family inventory
Audio, march, descent, and game-over timing targets

planTracks.title: Expand the artifact inventory before coding
planTracks.goal: Give the first runtime slice enough evidence that it feels deliberate rather than memory-driven.
deliverables: Still-image references for title, bunkers, cannon, and invader rows

nextArtifacts.title: Original score-table still or manual-page capture
nextArtifacts.why: We now have good written rules and scoring prose, but we still want stronger original-source score-table evidence before locking visible Windigo scoring claims.
find: Manual page with readable invader/UFO values
Attract or cabinet still showing score values
Photographic score-table reference
nextArtifacts.goodEnough: One readable original or near-original source that visually confirms the main score table.

nextArtifacts.title: Higher-resolution gameplay with cleaner audio
nextArtifacts.why: The current seed source is useful, but a clearer capture will help study bunker wear, march tempo, shot tone, and timing with more confidence.
find: Higher-resolution full gameplay video
Gameplay with less compression or cleaner original sound
Attract/start sequence footage
nextArtifacts.goodEnough: One second longplay or focused video that is visibly clearer than the first seed.

nextArtifacts.title: Cabinet, bezel, and title stills
nextArtifacts.why: We need to separate classic Space Invaders presentation truth from future Windigo theming, and that starts with clear visual reference.
find: Cabinet photos
Marquee or bezel art
Title/start or score strip stills
nextArtifacts.goodEnough: At least one clear image each for title/HUD and cabinet-facing presentation.

nextArtifacts.title: Bunker, cannon, and invader-row close references
nextArtifacts.why: Gameplay video alone is enough to start, but not enough to confidently define row-family identity, bunker damage reading, and cannon silhouette choices.
find: Close stills of bunkers
Close stills of the player cannon
Readable invader-row images or manual illustrations
nextArtifacts.goodEnough: A small still bundle that covers the player object, bunker, and all three main invader families.

nextArtifacts.title: Event-labeled timing clips for fire, hit, descent, and game over
nextArtifacts.why: These are the slices we will need for the first serious Platinum timing and audio conformance work.
find: Game start and first control handoff
Projectile fire and hit moments
Alien descent or acceleration windows
Player loss and game-over windows
nextArtifacts.goodEnough: 4-6 named windows with timestamps or preserved extracted clips.
